data.title: Are Hong Kong teachers, healthcare and social workers more alert to child abuse?
data.description:
Hong Kong has seen a rise in the number of potential child abuse cases reported by healthcare, education and social workers in recent years, with such professionals accounting for nearly 20 per cent of calls to a hotline, an NGO has said.
Against Child Abuse chairman Patrick Cheung Chi-hung attributed the continuous rise in calls by professionals from the healthcare, education and social sectors since 2022-23 to the gazetting of a child abuse mandatory reporting law.
“In 2024, the mandatory reporting ordinance was gazetted. For one or two years before that, there was heated debate in the Legislative Council; it raised a lot of awareness among professionals,” he said.
The Mandatory Reporting of Child Abuse Ordinance was passed in July last year, and is expected to come into effect in January 2026.
Under the new law, 25 categories of professionals encompassing teachers, social workers, doctors, among others, will be required to report any suspected cases of child abuse.
The organisation said its hotline received 1,144 calls in 2024-25, with 207 suspected cases of child abuse involving 241 youngsters arising from the reports.
Among the calls, 738, or about 64 per cent, were made by a child’s family members or, in a few cases, the alleged victims themselves.
There were 559 calls from the respective children’s mothers, 102 from fathers, 62 from other relatives and two calls dialled by children suspected of being abused.
Other callers include neighbours of suspected victims, members of the public, professionals, carers and even alleged abusers.
Professionals accounted for the second-largest proportion of calls to the hotline, at 211, or around 18 per cent.
The number of calls from professionals in 2024-25 also marked an increase from the 183 logged in 2023-24 and the 80 recorded in 2022-23.

The NGO’s director, Maple Lau Siu-kwan, said the hotline received an inquiry from a kindergarten teacher concerned about whether a child with unsatisfactory hygiene levels could be a victim of abuse.
The director said her staff had worked together with teachers to follow up with the case, identifying the support needed for the family involved, adding that no child abuse was uncovered.
“Our colleagues have noticed that professionals may not clearly know the definition and signs of child abuse. Some who know a bit more might call us to inquire [if abuse has happened] once they noticed some signs,” Lau said.
A breakdown of the 2024-25 call tally also showed 189 calls, or 17 per cent, were made by members of the public or neighbours.
Its chairman, Cheung, said: “For parents or members of the public, in general, they are [calling over] child management problems or how to avoid marital problems spilling over onto their children.”
In 2024-25, the NGO made 20 referrals to other organisations for suspected cases reported to its hotline or via drop-in services at its centres.
Six were made to the Social Welfare Department, two to police, three to social services at schools, eight to integrated family service centres and one to another NGO.
In terms of the ages of suspected victims, children six to eight years old made up the largest proportion, with the age group accounting for 58 cases, or 24 per cent.
This was followed by children aged three to five, at 22 per cent, and those between nine and 11, at 16 per cent.
According to the NGO’s figures, 116 of the alleged child abuse cases were categorised as suspected physical abuse, 34 as suspected neglect, 26 as suspected mental abuse, 22 as involving more than one form of alleged abuse and nine as suspected sexual abuse.
Cheung said that going forward, interdisciplinary training uniting different categories of professionals would be more effective in enhancing collaboration and communication when handling suspected cases of child abuse.

data.title: Chinese tech giants from Kuaishou to Tencent reap early gains from big AI bets
data.description:
Chinese technology powerhouses are beginning to reap early returns from their heavy investments in artificial intelligence, with quarterly results showing the impact across businesses from short video platforms like Kuaishou Technology to social media giants like Tencent Holdings.
Kuaishou, China’s second-largest short video app and rival to TikTok’s Chinese sibling Douyin, was the latest to highlight gains from AI adoption. The company, in an earnings statement on Wednesday, attributed its third-quarter revenue growth to the use of AI technology to upgrade marketing solutions and its Kling video tool.
Online marketing services sales grew 14 per cent to 20.1 billion yuan (US$2.81 billion), while sales for other services jumped 41.3 per cent to 5.9 billion yuan in the three months ended September.
Kuaishou co-founder and CEO Cheng Yixiao said in the earnings statement that “AI capabilities deeply embedded across business scenarios” helped it deliver a robust quarter.

Tencent, the Chinese social media and video gaming powerhouse, posted a 19 per cent jump in third-quarter profit on AI-related gains, it said last week. Pony Ma Huateng, founder, chairman and CEO of Tencent, said areas from ad targeting to game engagement to producing code, games and videos benefited from strategic investments in AI.
AI also emerged as the rare bright spot for Baidu, which posted an underwhelming quarter on Tuesday. The search engine company saw a 50 per cent increase in AI-related revenue across its cloud, application and marketing businesses in the third quarter, contrasting with a 7 per cent decline in overall revenue during the period.
Analysts at BOC International said the wider use of AI tools across Chinese firms had shown positive effects on business performance. “AI applications in specific business scenarios have been validated with emerging monetisation potential,” the securities firm said in a note on Wednesday.
China’s Big Tech companies have been spending heavily on AI to empower businesses and enhance efficiency. But there are also concerns about excessive spending and the risk of wasted investment.
Brock Silvers, chief investment officer at private equity investment firm Kaiyuan Capital, said the scale of capital expenditure “should be extremely worrisome to investors” at a time when AI’s potentials have not been fully realised.
Silvers added that “should AI disappoint, or merely display a longer timeline, market reaction could suddenly turn savage”.
China’s tech executives, meanwhile, remain confident in AI. Kuaishou’s Cheng said in a post-earnings conference call that “in the long run [we] believe a comprehensive AI application ecosystem will help the company better adjust to the market with high growth potential”.
Baidu chief financial officer He Haijian said the company’s AI-related businesses had “laid a solid foundation for sustainable long-term growth”.
Charlie Chai, deputy head of research consultancy 86Research, said Chinese firms’ disciplined return-on-investment culture would allow them to keep creating value during the AI boom while cushioning against any downturn if the bubble bursts.

data.title: 13 killed in Cambodia bus crash near Angkor Wat en route to Phnom Penh
data.description:
A passenger bus in Cambodia crashed off a bridge into a river on Thursday, killing at least 13 passengers and injuring two dozen others, police said.
The bus was travelling from Siem Reap, home to the country’s fabled Angkor Wat temple complex, to the capital Phnom Penh, when it crashed in the predawn hours in the central province of Kampong Thom, the deputy police chief for the area, Siv Sovanna, said.
All those on the bus were Cambodian nationals, he said.
A preliminary investigation suggested that the driver had been drowsy after his nighttime departure from Siem Reap, for what is normally about a 5½ hour trip, Siv Sovanna said. He did not say if the driver was among the dead.
There were believed to have been about 40 passengers who boarded the bus, and rescuers continued to search on Thursday for any more victims.
The bodies of the dead would be kept at a hospital near the site of the crash until they could be picked up by their relatives, he said.
Traffic crashes in Cambodia killed 1,509 people in 2024, while 1,062 people were killed in the first nine months of 2025 in the country, according to the Ministry of Public Works and Transport.

data.title: China is building the world’s first floating artificial island with nuclear-proof bunker
data.description:
China is building a mega science infrastructure that could add fuel to the global maritime power race: a floating artificial island engineered to withstand nuclear blasts.
This 78,000-tonne, semi-submersible twin-hull platform is world’s first mobile, self-sustaining artificial island.
With a displacement rivalling the People’s Liberation Army Navy’s new Fujian aircraft carrier and the capacity to house 238 occupants for four months without resupply, the facility will be able to project unprecedented power across contested oceans when it enters service in 2028.
But beneath its scientific veneer lies a rare nuclear-blast-resistant design that uses “metamaterial” sandwich panels to turn catastrophic shocks into gentle squeezes, according to scientists involved in this project.
Why would a scientific facility need to withstand a nuclear blast?
“This deep-sea major scientific facility is designed for all-weather, long-term residency,” wrote the team led by Professor Yang Deqing with Shanghai Jiao Tong University (SJTU) in a peer-reviewed paper published in Chinese Journal of Ship Research on November 4.
“Its superstructure contains critical compartments that ensure emergency power, communications and navigation control – making nuclear blast protection for these spaces absolutely vital.”
Officially named the Deep-Sea All-Weather Resident Floating Research Facility and designated as a national major scientific infrastructure project in China’s 14th five-year plan, the facility – also dubbed a “far-sea floating mobile island” – comes after a decade’s research and planning, according to SJTU.


According to the university’s design contracts signed in December 2024 with China State Shipbuilding Corporation, the platform will measure 138 metres long and 85 metres wide (125 feet long and 279 feet wide), with a main deck rising 45 metres above the waterline.
Its twin-hull design allows it to operate in sea state 7 (rough seas with 6-9 metre waves) while surviving typhoons up to category 17 – the most powerfully rated tropical cyclones on Earth.
What makes this platform revolutionary is its mobility combined with permanence. Unlike fixed research stations or conventional vessels limited by fuel and supplies, this floating island can cruise at 15 knots while supporting over a hundred researchers conducting continuous deep-sea observations, testing next-generation marine equipment and exploring seabed mining technologies.
“We’re racing to complete the design and construction, aiming for operational status by 2028,” said Lin Zhongqin, an academician leading the project in an interview with Economic Information Daily in April last year.
The facility promises to become a “paradigm shift” in ocean science, offering real-scale testing capabilities that no laboratory or existing ship can match.
The facility’s intended operating areas potentially include disputed maritime zones such as the South China Sea. While officially a civilian research platform, its design references GJB 1060.1-1991, a Chinese military specification for nuclear blast resistance, indicating planners are preparing for the worst-case scenario of nuclear attacks.


But the challenge is formidable, according to Yang’s team.
A nuclear explosion generates a supersonic shock wave that delivers a devastating combination of reflected overpressure and dynamic pressure. Traditional protection relies on massive steel plates – thick, heavy and space-consuming. For a facility prioritising weight efficiency and interior volume, conventional armour is impractical.
Yang and his colleagues propose an elegant solution: a “sandwich bulkhead”.
It is a three-dimensional “negative Poisson’s ratio” corrugated tube structure – essentially a microscopic lattice of folded metal tubes arranged in a precise geometric pattern. This structure works like a magical sponge. When squeezed, it contracts inward, becoming denser and stronger.
But the real genius is what engineers call “quasi-static conversion”. When a nuclear blast hits conventional armour, the energy arrives as a violent spike – a hammer blow that peaks instantly then fades.
This new metamaterial structure, however, behaves like a sophisticated crumple zone. As the shock wave compresses the corrugated tubes, they fold in a controlled, sequential manner, spreading the impact over time and space.
Instead of a hammer blow, the structure experiences a steady, manageable push – what engineers describe as transforming a “dynamic” shock into a “quasi-static” squeeze.
Think of it like catching a fastball barehanded versus using a baseball glove.
The researchers optimised this design through thousands of computer simulations, determining that tubes with a 21.25-degree angle and 0.6-mm (0.02 inches) wall thickness provided the sweet spot: maximum energy absorption with minimal peak force.
The result is a sandwich panel just 60mm (2.4 inches) thick – about the width of a smartphone – that outperforms much heavier steel plates.
Under simulated nuclear blast loading equivalent to 177.83 kilopascals of overpressure – enough to level most buildings – the metamaterial sandwich bulkhead reduced maximum displacement by 58.53 per cent compared to conventional design.


The peak stress dropped by 14.25 per cent, keeping the structure within elastic limits and preventing permanent deformation. Critically, the new design weighs slightly less than the conventional armour and occupies less space, solving the weight-volume paradox that has long plagued protective engineering.
The facility’s potential applications could extend far beyond pure research, according to some military observers. Unlike land reclamation projects – which are permanent, environmentally controversial and diplomatically sensitive – a mobile floating island offers presence without permanence.
It can be deployed to contested waters for research, maintain a continuous presence and relocate as political situations evolve – all at lower cost and environmental impact than building artificial islands.
The facility could serve multiple functions as a resilient command node, a logistics hub or a surveillance station. Its 120-day endurance without resupply exceeds that of many nuclear-powered aircraft carriers, offering persistent presence in remote areas.
The reference to military blast standards in civilian design specifications suggests dual-use capability is being built in from the ground up.
The facility also represents China’s ambition to lead in “blue economy” technologies: deep-sea resource extraction, marine renewable energy and climate change research.
The South China Sea contains valuable mineral deposits and biodiversity that remain largely unexplored. A self-sustaining research platform could unlock discoveries impossible from shore-based labs or short-duration ship expeditions.

data.title: As power balance tips to China, world needs a fairer financial system
data.description:
This month, the Russian Academy of Sciences completed a study assessing the comprehensive strength of 193 countries across economic, technological, demographic, military, infrastructural and other dimensions. This annual assessment of national power is conducted using advanced methods of multivariate statistical analysis.
The latest results, calculated for 2025 and 2026, show the global balance of power has finally shifted from the United States towards China. Also, several regional power cores have emerged, including Russia and India, which are becoming potential centres of global geopolitics. The study shows China strengthening further, the US losing ground, and Russia and India consolidating their roles as regional power centres, while Germany faces a structural decline in competitiveness.
But the rapidly strengthening macro region led by China has encountered a constraint in the form of the global system’s financial core. At its centre remains the US dollar, which functions as the main economic instrument of American influence and allows the US to sustain debt-driven growth at the expense of the rest of the world.
In practice, these are unsecured debt obligations that circulate the globe as foreign reserves and units of settlement, while the resulting inflation returns not only to the US but also to all countries that hold dollar assets. The US Federal Reserve is finding it increasingly difficult to support the economy and contain inflation at the same time, and the contradiction between the global role of the dollar and the sustainability of its issuer is becoming more apparent.
The key issue, however, lies elsewhere. For China and the emerging macro region to continue their growth, it is essential to achieve greater financial sovereignty and to expand both existing and new trade routes denominated in yuan. This would reduce dependence on the Federal Reserve’s policy strategy and further strengthen China’s weight in the global economic system.
It should also be remembered that as international use of the yuan expands and the US dollar share declines, the relative position of the US will inevitably weaken. For decades, the US has financed its trade deficit through additional dollar issuance, creating an imbalance in which some nations produce real goods while others issue paper claims.

In this context, it is particularly important that China has launched its Cross-border Interbank Payment System (CIPS), regarded on the one hand as an alternative to the Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication (SWIFT) network, and on the other as a mechanism that promotes wider yuan use in the global financial system. In essence, it represents a quiet financial revolution that is shaping the contours of the world economy.
While the Trump administration’s US national security strategy has not yet been released, it is worth examining a document by the Rand Corporation, a think tank closely connected with the Pentagon and US Congress, and regularly commissioned by the government to study key issues of national security. In its October 14 report, “Stabilising the US-China Rivalry”, Rand explored the possibility of easing US-China tensions, reflecting a desire to avoid a direct military confrontation and breakdown of the global order.
The main conclusion of the report, which has since been withdrawn “for further review”, was that Washington should move away from rhetoric focused on achieving an “absolute” victory over China and instead seek a long-term balance that would allow peaceful coexistence between the two systems.
It also emphasised the importance of strengthening unofficial channels of communication, including between the military and political communities of both countries, which could help prevent crises even when official diplomatic contacts weaken. The document does not represent an attempt to reconcile with China, but rather the perspective of those within the American establishment who recognise the red lines of confrontation.
What matters most for the near future, and what the national power indicators mentioned at the beginning clearly show, are that economic and financial strength is becoming the dominant factor in the international agenda. It is therefore essential to focus on building an independent financial framework.


While the US searches for ways to adapt at the geopolitical level, similar efforts are already taking place within the International Monetary Fund. A new IMF study, “Navigating the 2022 Inflation Surge: A Comparative Analysis of IT and Non-IT Central Banks”, published on October 24, provides an important argument in this context and marks, without exaggeration, a new milestone in the evolution of monetary doctrine.
The report effectively acknowledges that the regime of inflation targeting, which for decades had been promoted worldwide as a universal and effective tool for price stabilisation, failed to deliver during the global inflation shock of 2022.
These conclusions are based on an analysis of 70 countries, including 33 that pursued inflation targeting and 37 that did not. The sample covers both developed and developing economies. The main takeaway is that the era of dogmatic inflation targeting has reached the limits of its effectiveness.
As recent experience shows, the world no longer operates according to outdated textbooks. Rising energy prices, disrupted supply chains and ongoing military conflicts have shown that mechanical policy models have lost their ability to control outcomes and cannot protect economies from turbulence.
In this context, the independence of monetary policy and genuine partnership within a fairer financial and trade framework among the countries of the emerging macro region will support sustainable development in the short and long term. Financial multipolarity is becoming the natural extension of geopolitical multipolarity. Nations that can combine production capacity, technological autonomy and their own currency infrastructure are shaping the contours of a more stable global order.

data.title: Trump targets Disney’s ABC over Khashoggi question, Kimmel comments
data.description:
The White House ratcheted up its attack on ABC News Wednesday, a day after US President Donald Trump criticised an ABC News correspondent for asking Saudi Arabia’s crown prince about the 2018 killing of a Washington Post columnist.
The fresh critique came as the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) said on Wednesday it was opening a review of agreements between national networks and local broadcast stations.
Trump suggested the commission should move to revoke the broadcast licenses of Disney-owned ABC stations following questions about Jamal Khashoggi that he called “insubordinate”.

FCC Chair Brendan Carr, a Republican, said the review will cover when stations can opt not to air programming on public interest grounds. The FCC, an independent federal agency, issues eight-year licenses to individual broadcast stations, not networks.
The White House press office described ABC News as “a Democratic spin operation masquerading as a broadcast network” in an email sent to the press, which included a list of grievances against the network dating from 2017. It accused ABC News of waging “war” on the president and the millions of Americans who elected him to office.
Disney-owned ABC News declined comment.
In September, Carr praised two major broadcast owners, Sinclair Broadcast Group and Nexstar Media Group, who briefly opted not to air Jimmy Kimmel Live! on their 70 ABC-affiliated stations covering nearly a quarter of US households. Nexstar needs FCC approval to acquire Tegna in a US$3.54 billion deal and on Tuesday formally submitted an application to the agency.

ABC briefly suspended Kimmel’s show on September 17 over comments he made about the assassination of conservative activist Charlie Kirk. Hours before the suspension, Carr warned that local broadcasters who aired Kimmel could face fines or loss of licenses and said “it’s time for them to step up”. Carr came under bipartisan criticism for those comments and will appear before the Senate Commerce Committee on December 17.
Late on Wednesday, Trump once again escalated his criticism of Kimmel and ABC in a social media post, calling on the broadcast network to get Kimmel off the air.
Contracts between the networks and affiliates have penalties for not airing programming for extended periods. The FCC notice asked, “Are national programmers able to take actions or threaten to punish local broadcast TV stations that attempt to exercise their lawful right to pre-empt national programming?”
The agency asked if it should adopt regulations to address “anticompetitive leverage and behaviour by large networks?”
Carr said the FCC had initiated the review because national programmers were “reportedly preventing those broadcasters from serving their local communities – including by punishing them for exercising their right to pre-empt national programming”.

Last December, ABC News agreed to give US$15 million to the Trump presidential library to resolve a lawsuit over comments that anchor George Stephanopoulos made on air involving the civil case brought against Trump by writer E. Jean Carroll.
Carr has taken a series of steps to investigate media companies. In July, he opened a probe into NBC-parent Comcast’s relationships with its local broadcast TV affiliates.
The FCC in July approved the US$8.4 billion merger between CBS parent Paramount Global and Skydance Media after Skydance agreed to ensure CBS news and entertainment programming is free of bias and end diversity programmes.
The approval came weeks after Paramount agreed to pay US$16 million to settle Trump’s lawsuit against CBS over its editing of a 60 Minutes interview with his Democratic presidential opponent, Kamala Harris.

data.title: SJM cancels Ponte 16 acquisition, but pursues deal for L’Arc Macau
data.description:
Integrated resorts operator SJM Holdings, one of the six licensed casino concessionaires in Macau, has abandoned a plan to acquire Ponte 16, which is among the firm’s nine satellite gambling venues directed to halt operations under the city’s revised gaming laws.
“Following a comprehensive business review and after a thorough assessment of long-term business planning, commercial considerations and resource prioritisation across the group’s portfolio, SJM Resorts will not proceed with the acquisition,” the Hong Kong-listed company’s principal subsidiary said in a statement on Thursday.
In June, SJM Holdings said it would shut seven of its satellite casinos this year and buy out the gambling halls of Ponte 16 and L’Arc Macau before directly managing them.
That plan was drawn up in response to the Macau government’s amended gaming laws in 2022, which gave the satellite casinos three years until December 31, 2025, to shift from a profit-sharing model to a structure where they are directly owned by the licensed concessionaires.
SJM Resorts on Wednesday entered into a termination agreement with Pier 16 – Entertainment Group Corp. SJM Resorts held a 51 per cent stake in Pier 16.
Cancelling the acquisition of Ponte 16 was a tactical move for SJM Holdings after it reported last week a 91 per cent fall in third-quarter net profit from a year earlier.

SJM Holdings’ gaming revenue also declined 6.5 per cent last quarter, as some of its satellite casinos were closed. It was the only one among Macau’s six licensed casino operators that posted lower revenue during the period.
The company, whose flagship properties include the Grand Lisboa Palace Resort Macau and the Casino Grand Lisboa, also held debt totalling HK$27.3 billion as of the end of September.
Shares of SJM Holdings gained 0.37 per cent to close at HK$2.71 on Thursday.
Still, SJM Resorts has proposed to acquire the entire stake that it does not own in Arc of Triumph Development Company, whose principal assets include L’Arc Hotel in Macau, and to arrange the repayment of a bank loan amounting to HK$1.75 billion. The hotel is currently operated by SJM Resorts under a services agreement.
The Arc of Triumph firm is indirectly owned by Angela Leong On-kei, the fourth spouse of the late casino tycoon and SJM Holdings founder Stanley Ho Hung-sun. Leong also serves as co-chairman and executive director at SJM Holdings.
Meanwhile, Ponte 16 will officially cease operations at midnight on November 28. All its gaming tables and gaming machines will be redeployed to other casinos under SJM Resorts. Employees of Ponte 16 who were not hired by SJM Resorts would be invited to apply for related open positions within the company and provided with necessary support to facilitate a smooth transition, according to the company’s statement.

data.title: China Future Tech webinar: how the Nexperia saga will change supply-chain considerations
data.description:
While the Dutch government has offered goodwill in the dispute over control of Nexperia, a Chinese-owned, Netherlands-based chipmaker, the crisis is far from over.
China’s Ministry of Commerce on Wednesday welcomed the Dutch government’s revocation of an executive order taking control of the firm. But it also noted that a Dutch court ruling remained a “key obstacle” to resolving the matter. Wingtech Technology, the Chinese owner of Nexperia, said it had yet to resume control of the chipmaker.
In the latest China Future Tech webinar on Thursday, the Post’s Xiaofei Xu, political economy reporter based in Paris, and Coco Feng, senior tech reporter based in southern China’s Guangdong province, discussed the likely development of the dispute and its broad implications.


What is Wingtech likely to do to regain control of Nexperia, and how likely is it to succeed?
Wingtech could continue the legal battle by going to the Dutch supreme court to overthrow the Enterprise Chamber ruling on October 7 that kicked Zhang Xuezheng, the former CEO, off the board of directors. While there is a slim chance that a review by the court would overthrow the Enterprise Chamber decision, it could take months or even years before a final decision is made.
Will Nexperia be separated into two separate parts, and how would this affect auto chip supplies?
A likely scenario is that Nexperia China will be run by a group separate from the management team at the headquarters in Nijmegen.
Nexperia China would comprise the packaging factory in Dongguan, Guangdong province, which accounted for 70 per cent of Nexperia’s output, as well as its sales and supporting facilities on the mainland. The non-China operations, including the wafer-fabrication facilities in Europe and two other packaging plants in Asia, would be run out of the Netherlands.
The two sides have been quarrelling over a range of issues, blaming each other for the supply-chain disruptions. The situation is expected to continue until the two sides can compromise. It will be up to the negotiations between the two separate operations, as well as China’s export control relaxations, to decide whether stable supplies can be guaranteed.
What are the key implications of the Nexperia saga on both sides?
Europeans, especially European politicians, have to think about risks and geopolitical tensions in approving Chinese investment deals. European governments are expected to emphasise “autonomy” from both the US and China.
For Chinese businesses, the seizure of Nexperia is a cautionary tale. They must be alert to the geopolitical risks of investing abroad. Chinese investors are now asking companies about investment risks in Europe after the Nexperia dispute.

data.title: China-US-Russia rivalry for Middle East stealth fighter sales on show in Dubai
data.description:
An angular F-35 fighter jet penetrates the bright blue sky over a dusty desert, its engines roaring as it goes through a series of high-speed, gravity-defying turns and high-G manoeuvres before disappearing over the horizon.
About an hour later, another aircraft appears, this time a Russian Su-57 with a blended-wing design and slightly deeper engine noise that also displays its aerobatics and speed, showcasing its supersonic cruising ability before returning to the runway.

These two flights on the opening day of the Dubai Air Show in the United Arab Emirates on Monday marked the first time that American and Russian fifth-generation fighter jets have appeared at the same event.
Also on show was China’s fifth-generation J-35, in a static display at the booth of state-owned defence giant China National Aero-Technology Import & Export Corporation (Catic).
Analysts said the presence at the air show of the only three countries producing and delivering stealth jets to their own air forces showed how competition for the Middle East market had heated up.

In addition to stealth, these advanced aircraft typically feature supercruise, advanced sensor fusion and super manoeuvrability. They are equipped with integrated avionics and internal weapons bays.
Fifth-generation fighters also boast enhanced situational awareness through a networking capability that means they can operate more effectively in air-to-air and air-to-ground roles compared with older aircraft.
The air show, which concludes on Friday, coincided with the Donald Trump administration’s announcement of plans to sell F-35s to Saudi Arabia, confirmed on Wednesday during Crown Prince Mohammad bin Salman’s visit to the US.
Washington has previously rejected requests for F-35s from Saudi Arabia and the UAE because of US law mandating that Israel has a qualitative edge over its Arab adversaries, as well as technological security concerns.
Considered the most advanced of the jets, the F-35 has been restricted to key allies of the United States, with deliveries to 12 countries so far. In contrast, the Russian Su-57 and Chinese J-35 were only delivered to their air forces in 2020 and 2025, respectively.
According to observers, this year’s air show showed that Chinese systems were becoming increasingly competitive against their Russian counterparts, especially for countries barred from acquiring US weapons.
Timothy Heath, a senior international defence researcher at the US-based Rand Corporation, noted that the US was not the only country interested in selling the advanced aircraft in the Middle East.
“Russia is looking for clients as well, perhaps Iran, for the Su-57. The potential sales of fifth-generation aircraft to Middle Eastern countries show that there is a market for China’s J-35 as well,” he said.
“China is most competitive with Russia, since the purchase of fifth-generation aircraft is as much about politics as it is about hardware. Countries that buy from the United States, such as Israel, are less likely to buy from China or Russia.”
Heath added that Chinese hardware was becoming competitive against its Russian counterparts, with China’s technology and quality “equal or possibly superior” to Russia’s in many cases.




Liselotte Odgaard, a senior fellow at the Hudson Institute in Washington, said the J-35 was likely to be a “desirable option” for countries unable to afford the F-35 or that might encounter disapproval from the US.
“The J-35 is more sophisticated than Russia’s fifth-generation aircraft and sells at a competitive price. Consequently, seen from a US perspective, the Chinese offer is the biggest competitor in the Middle East market,” Odgaard said.
“However, there are caveats to the J-35, such as uncertainty regarding the Chinese aircraft’s software – for example, sensor-fusion software and network-centric integration.”
“China is a fairly new actor in the market for technologically highly advanced platforms and does not have a track record in upgrades, maintenance and so on that shows its long-term reliability.”
Gareth Jennings, aviation editor at global military intelligence company Janes, said the presence of the US and Russian stealth fighters was part of a decades-long “wider geopolitical competition for influence and market share in the Middle East”.
“While the F-35 hasn’t yet been made available to Middle East customers – notwithstanding an earlier now-lapsed approval for the UAE and reports of a potential sale to Saudi Arabia – its showcasing at the Dubai air show underscores the US commitment to the region while reinforcing its technological edge.”


The Su-57 made its international debut last year at China’s Zhuhai air show. Meanwhile, the J-35 was seen in action in official videos released in September showing sea trials for the Fujian aircraft carrier.
The Chinese stealth fighter, which like the F-35 was designed for aircraft carriers, was shown conducting catapult take-off manoeuvres from the Fujian, making it the first in the world to successfully launch from a carrier using the advanced technology.
According to Odgaard, the sale of fifth-generation fighter jets in the Middle East would constitute an “important change” in air superiority and provide an indicator of political alignment.
“In a military sense, the fifth-generation fighter jets are game changers because they offer stealth, advanced sensor fusion and networked operations that provide potentially decisive advantages over older aircraft such as F-15s,” she said.
“Geopolitically, the choice of a jet also signals countries’ political-strategic alignment.”

data.title: Princess Diana enters Paris waxwork museum in ‘revenge dress’
data.description:
The Grevin waxwork museum in Paris unveiled a new star attraction on Thursday: Princess Diana in the “revenge dress” she wore after public revelations about her then-husband Prince Charles’s infidelity.
The Grevin Museum in central Paris, similar to Madame Tussauds in London, already has models of Charles, who is now King Charles, and his late mother, Queen Elizabeth.
But Diana was a notable absentee, despite her tragic association with the city where she died in a car crash in August 1997.
She is displayed wearing a copy of the black gown by designer Christina Stambolian that she wore for a public appearance in 1994 amid a media frenzy about the breakdown of her marriage to Charles.
She stepped out in the dazzling off-the-shoulder dress on the same day as an interview was broadcast in which Charles admitted to being unfaithful.

“More than 28 years after her tragic death in Paris, Diana is still a major figure in global pop culture, celebrated for her style, humanity and independence,” the Grevin Museum said in a statement.
“The gown became a statement of reclaimed self-assertion, a powerful image of determined femininity and renewed confidence,” it added.
Her waxwork is displayed far from King Charles and Queen Elizabeth, who are in a gallery for heads of state, with the late “Lady Di” standing alongside fashion and entertainment figures such as Jean Paul Gaultier and Franco-Malian singing star Aya Nakamura.
The date chosen for the unveiling – November 20 – was also a “sly reference” to a bombshell interview she gave to the BBC on the same date exactly thirty years ago in which she said “there were three of us in this marriage, so it was a little bit crowded”.
That was a reference to Camilla Parker-Bowles, whom Charles has since married.
The high-pressure waxwork commission – which will be highly scrutinised by defenders of Diana’s memory – was handed to Paris-based sculptor Laurent Mallamaci.
The Grevin Museum had been in contact with Diana at the end of her life but abandoned the idea of making a figure of her after her death, a spokesperson said.
